<findings>

[observation] [info] High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
[observation] [info] Because there is no package entrypoint, downstream code may reach into internal files—publish a typed index to protect encapsulation.
[observation] [info] Lack of smoke tests per provider leaves config/auth regressions undetected; add minimal CLI-to-provider test coverage.
[observation] [info] Provider abstractions are extremely cohesive (1.0) with zero outward dependencies, showing a cleanly isolated client SDK layer.

</findings>

<insights>

- High cohesion (1) — files are tightly interconnected
- Provider adapters share config/auth helpers, so enforcing a single export surface (e.g., `create-client.ts`) keeps CLI consumers stable.
- There is no explicit entrypoint; documenting the public API barrel would reduce accidental deep imports.
- Add lightweight contract tests for each provider to ensure feature flags like exec/help formatting remain consistent across backends.
- Provider abstractions are extremely cohesive (1.0) with zero outward dependencies, showing a cleanly isolated client SDK layer.
- Because there is no package entrypoint, downstream code may reach into internal files—publish a typed index to protect encapsulation.
- Lack of smoke tests per provider leaves config/auth regressions undetected; add minimal CLI-to-provider test coverage.
- [call graph] 360 internal calls, 0 outgoing, 0 incoming (cohesion: 1, coupling: 0)

</insights>
